united_kingdom.name: British Empire
united_kingdom.summary:
Britain stands at the dawn of global dominance. The Industrial Revolution, ignited in the mills and mines of northern England, is giving Britain an economic and military edge that no other nation can yet match. Steam power, coal, iron, and textile machinery are transforming the world economy — and Britain controls all of them.

The loss of the American colonies (1783) has been more than compensated by gains in India, where the East India Company controls Bengal and is extending its reach across the subcontinent. Australia has just been settled. The Napoleonic Wars are reshaping Europe, and Britain's Royal Navy — the most powerful fleet in the world — guarantees British commercial dominance on every ocean.
united_kingdom.population_estimate: ~10 million in Britain; ~200 million under influence or control
key_events: Industrial Revolution transforms Britain into the world's manufacturing center
Battle of the Nile (1798) — Nelson destroys French fleet, securing Mediterranean
East India Company controls Bengal and expands across India
Abolition movement gains strength — slave trade will be abolished in 1807
united_kingdom.did_you_know: At its peak in the early 20th century, the British Empire covered roughly 25% of the world's land surface and governed about 20% of the world's population — the largest empire in human history. In 1800, this was just beginning.

france.name: Napoleonic France
france.summary:
Napoleon Bonaparte has seized power through the coup of 18 Brumaire (November 1799) and made himself First Consul of France. He will crown himself Emperor in 1804. Emerging from the chaos of the French Revolution, Napoleon channels revolutionary energy outward, conquering most of continental Europe and reshaping its legal and political landscape.

The Napoleonic Code — a comprehensive legal system Napoleon imposed across conquered Europe — abolished feudalism, established equality before the law, and protected property rights. Its influence persists in the legal systems of dozens of countries today. Napoleon's battles are the most studied in military history, and the era he defined — the Napoleonic Era — reshaped Europe's map and political imagination.
france.ruler: Napoleon Bonaparte, First Consul (r. 1799–1804), then Emperor (1804–1814/15)
key_events: Coup of 18 Brumaire (1799) — Napoleon seizes power
Battle of Marengo (1800) secures Napoleon's rule
Napoleonic Code drafted — revolutionary legal reforms spread across Europe
Louisiana Purchase (1803) — Napoleon sells French North America to the U.S.
france.did_you_know: Napoleon was not short — this is a British propaganda myth. Contemporary records show he was about 5'7" (170 cm), average height for the era. The confusion arose partly from a difference between French and English measurement units, and partly from British caricatures deliberately depicting him as tiny.

qing_empire.name: Qing Dynasty
qing_empire.summary:
The Qing Dynasty governs the largest China has ever been — including Tibet, Mongolia, Xinjiang, and Manchuria alongside the traditional Han heartland. Emperor Qianlong (just retired in 1799) presided over a century of expansion and prosperity, and China in 1800 still produces approximately 30% of world GDP — more than all of Europe combined.

However, storm clouds are gathering. The population has exploded from 150 million to 300 million in a century, straining agricultural systems. Corruption plagues the bureaucracy. A British trade mission in 1793 under Lord Macartney was famously turned away by the emperor, who declared China had no need for 'barbarian' products — a decision that reflects the self-confidence and the blind spot of the late Qing. The Opium Wars are four decades away.
key_events: Qing Empire at its largest territorial extent under Qianlong
Lord Macartney's trade mission rejected (1793) — a fateful decision
White Lotus Rebellion (1796–1804) signals growing internal stress
China produces ~30% of world GDP but is about to fall behind rapidly
qing_empire.did_you_know: In 1800, China was the world's largest economy, producing roughly 30% of global GDP — comparable to the United States today. Within a century, repeated wars, unequal treaties, and internal upheaval reduced China to a fraction of its former economic weight. This 'century of humiliation' profoundly shapes Chinese identity today.

ottoman_empire.name: Ottoman Empire
ottoman_empire.summary:
The Ottoman Empire in 1800 is vast but weakening — often called 'the sick man of Europe,' though this phrase won't appear for another fifty years. Provinces are increasingly autonomous, janissaries (the elite troops) have become a political force resistant to reform, and European powers are beginning to encroach on Ottoman territory.

Napoleon's invasion of Egypt in 1798 — technically Ottoman territory — exposed the empire's inability to defend its periphery. The Ottomans are beginning to recognize they must modernize, but every reform attempt triggers conservative backlash. Sultan Selim III is attempting military modernization (Nizam-ı Cedid), but will be overthrown by janissaries resisting change.
key_events: Napoleon invades Egypt (1798) — exposing Ottoman weakness
Selim III attempts military reform (Nizam-ı Cedid) against janissary resistance
Greek independence movement is beginning to stir
Wahhabi movement in Arabia challenges Ottoman religious authority
ottoman_empire.did_you_know: The Ottoman Empire's decline took over a century — a remarkably slow collapse for what critics called a dying state. At its end in 1923, the Ottoman Empire had outlasted nearly every contemporary state that had declared it finished. Its successor, modern Turkey, emerged from the wreckage under Mustafa Kemal Atatürk.

united_states_of_america.name: United States
united_states_of_america.summary:
The United States in 1800 is a young republic of 16 states and approximately 5 million people, still clinging to the eastern seaboard. The first presidential transfer of power — from John Adams to Thomas Jefferson — is about to occur peacefully, a remarkable and non-obvious achievement for a new nation.

The Louisiana Purchase (1803) will double the country's size overnight. The great westward expansion is beginning. But slavery casts a shadow over the republic's founding ideals — the Constitution's compromise on slavery is a wound that will require a devastating civil war six decades later to begin to heal.
key_events: First peaceful transfer of presidential power — Adams to Jefferson (1801)
Washington D.C. becomes the new capital in 1800
Louisiana Purchase will double US territory (1803)
Cotton gin (1793) dramatically expands demand for enslaved labor in the South
united_states_of_america.did_you_know: When Washington D.C. became the capital in 1800, it was a swampy construction site with a population of about 3,000. The Capitol building was unfinished, the President's House (not yet called the White House) had just been built, and Pennsylvania Avenue was an unpaved path through mud.

russian_empire.name: Russian Empire
russian_empire.summary:
The Russian Empire under Tsar Paul I (and soon Alexander I) is the largest state in the world by land area, stretching from Poland to the Pacific. Peter the Great and Catherine the Great transformed Russia from a backward medieval state into a major European power over the previous century, adopting Western technology and culture while maintaining autocratic rule.

Russia is rapidly expanding in all directions: southward toward the Ottoman Empire and Persia, eastward across Siberia (Russian settlers reach Alaska), and westward into Poland and Finland. Napoleon is about to make the catastrophic mistake of invading Russia in 1812 — a campaign that will destroy his Grande Armée and reshape European history.
russian_empire.ruler: Tsar Paul I (r. 1796–1801); Alexander I from 1801
key_events: Russia expands to include Alaska (Russian America) and Central Asia
Catherine the Great's legacy — Westernization and territorial expansion
Napoleon's invasion (1812) will end in catastrophic French defeat
Russian culture — Pushkin, later Tolstoy — beginning to flourish
russian_empire.did_you_know: Russia sold Alaska to the United States in 1867 for $7.2 million — roughly 2 cents per acre. The Russians considered it a strategic liability too expensive to defend. When gold was discovered there in 1896 and oil in the 20th century, the transaction was retrospectively called 'Seward's Folly' — but the real folly was Russia's.

maratha_confederacy.name: Maratha Confederacy
maratha_confederacy.summary:
The Maratha Confederacy, emerging from the western Deccan in the 17th century, had become the dominant power in India after breaking the Mughal Empire. By 1800, the Marathas control most of the Indian subcontinent outside of British Bengal and the far south. Their cavalry, which once raided as far as Bengal and the Punjab, represents the most formidable Indian military force of the era.

However, the Marathas are weakening — internal divisions between the major Maratha chiefs (Peshwa, Sindhia, Holkar, Bhonsle) are crippling their ability to respond to the growing British threat. The Second and Third Anglo-Maratha Wars (1803–1805, 1817–1818) will break the Confederacy, and British dominance over India will be complete.
key_events: Marathas are the dominant power in India, controlling most of the subcontinent
Battle of Panipat (1761) — Maratha power checked by Afghan Ahmad Shah Durrani
British East India Company begins to encroach from Bengal
Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803–05) — Sindhia and Bhonsle defeated by British
maratha_confederacy.did_you_know: At the Battle of Assaye in 1803, a young British commander named Arthur Wellesley defeated a much larger Maratha army — a victory he later called his 'hardest fought' battle, even harder than Waterloo. Wellesley became the Duke of Wellington and defeated Napoleon 12 years later.
